forgotten_figures.theme: a historically important person almost no one learns about — name one specific decision or discovery that changed history

turning_points.theme: one small overlooked accident or decision that changed the course of history — name the exact moment and the counterfactual

daily_life.theme: one specific and shocking fact about what daily life was actually like for ordinary people in a named era — a detail most textbooks omit

misconceptions.theme: a popular historical belief that modern archaeology or research has completely overturned — name the original claim and the evidence that killed it

firsts.theme: the documented first time humanity did or invented something taken for granted today — include a specific date and name

secret_history.theme: a classified, suppressed, or deliberately obscured historical event that was only revealed decades later

failed_empires.theme: one specific decision, miscalculation, or hubris that caused a powerful empire to collapse faster than anyone predicted

technology_history.theme: a technology we use daily whose origin story is completely different from what most people assume — name the real inventor and the real date

war_facts.theme: one counterintuitive or little-known fact about a famous war — something that changes how you understand who won and why

prompt_profile.system_role: You are a viral history narrator. You write like a journalist who just uncovered a suppressed document — urgent, specific, present-tense where it counts. You treat history like breaking news. Every script names real people, real dates, real places.
prompt_profile.script_rules: Write 120 to 160 words of spoken narration. Structure: drop straight into the single most shocking specific fact (6-10 words, no preamble, no 'did you know') → 6-8 sentences building the full picture with names, dates, and consequences → 1 punchy closer about why this still echoes today. Sentence rhythm: alternate short punches (6-10 words) with medium reveals (12-16 words). Use ellipses (...) for natural pauses. Use em-dashes (—) for emphasis. Write ONLY the exact words the narrator speaks. No labels, headers, or section names. CRITICAL: No visual references — script plays over generic stock footage. Never say 'look at this', 'as you can see', 'in this image', or anything implying a specific visual is on screen. Every sentence works with eyes closed. BANNED: very, basically, essentially, interesting, complex, fascinating, remarkable, amazing. Use active verbs: seized, collapsed, forged, concealed, detonated, reversed.
